summary: McKenzie discloses a significant one-character bug in his open-source A/B testing framework A/Bingo that affected z-score calculations. The bug could potentially change whether test results were reported as statistically significant. He fixed it remotely from a Japanese cafe while stuck in Nagoya and pushed the fix to the repository. After re-running confidence tests on all his historical data, no experimental results actually changed. He commits to writing unit tests for the statistics code to prevent similar issues.
key_points: A one-character bug in A/Bingo's z-score calculation could affect statistical significance reporting
The bug fortuitously overstated significance of already-significant tests and understated insignificant ones, limiting real-world damage
No historical experimental results changed after recalculation with the corrected code
The incident highlights the importance of unit testing statistical code, which McKenzie commits to adding
money_quotes: The bug is all of one character long.
I have fixed the bug (via the Slicehost console, on a Japanese cafe Internet PC, because I am stuck in Nagoya today again).

While the numerical calculation of the z-score was borked, it was borked in a subtle enough fashion that most statistically significant tests will retain their statistical significance under the borked calculation.

My quick eyeball suggests that it causes BCC to overstate the significance of tests which are very significant and understate the significance of tests which are insignificant, which is a very fortuitous set of properties for a random bug to have in an A/B testing framework.
I have re-run statistical confidence tests for everything I've ever done for BCC that I still have data for, and no experimental results changed as a result of the error.
key_insight: A one-character bug in statistical code can potentially invalidate A/B test results, underscoring why statistical calculations need unit tests even when the rest of your framework works correctly.
